summary: Gruber dissects the ethics of web attribution after AllThingsD reported on iPad apps crossing 100,000 without properly crediting Federico Viticci of MacStories, who broke the story. He argues that while AllThingsD included a link, describing MacStories as merely 'an Apple enthusiast site' without naming it or its author was dismissive and disrespectful. He contrasts this with even worse behavior from CNET and Rolling Stone, who included no sourcing at all. Gruber explains that proper attribution serves both the creator (who deserves credit) and the reader (who deserves to know the source). He argues that Daring Fireball's link-forwarding model is more honest than the page-view-hoarding approach of larger publications. His core point is that reputation is not zero-sum, and AllThingsD's defensiveness made them look small.
key_points: Linking to a source is necessary but insufficient — proper attribution requires naming the author and publication
Describing MacStories as 'an Apple enthusiast site' and writing 'macstories.net' instead of 'MacStories' were deliberate, subtle diminishments
CNET and Rolling Stone were even worse, reporting the same news with zero sourcing or links whatsoever
Attribution serves readers as much as creators — bylines foster both accountability and recognition
Reputation is not zero-sum: crediting a smaller publication costs nothing and reflects well on the creditor
money_quotes: If he's not worth crediting by name or publication, why is his story worth re-reporting?

Not even including a link to the source of a story is dishonest. My problem with Fried's 'an Apple enthusiast site' attribution is more nuanced. That attribution, including a link, is not dishonest. But it is severely slanted, and it is disparaging.
Reputation is not a zero-sum game. Defensiveness is never flattering. AllThingsD is established and big, but they're the ones who come out of this looking small.
'Enthusiast site' is pejorative. Enthusiast implies that MacStories is produced by zealous hobbyists.
If the story was good enough to re-report, it was good enough to give Viticci name recognition for AllThingsD's readers.
Much of what I do here at Daring Fireball, on the other hand, is designed to throw traffic to stuff written elsewhere.
People come to Daring Fireball to find links to read elsewhere. People go to AllThingsD to read articles at AllThingsD.
key_insight: Proper web attribution requires naming the person and publication, not just embedding a link — anything less is a deliberate diminishment that reveals more about the creditor's insecurity than the source's credibility.
